text: The surgeon performs an emergency diagnostic laparoscopy, inserting a blunt trocar in the left upper quadrant. The laparoscopic view confirms severe small bowel distension, and additional trocars are placed to facilitate abdominal exploration.

The surgeon confirms the presence of ileocecal valve cancer. Adhesions from a previous open collar cystectomy elevate the right colon towards the abdominal wall. The view is obstructed by extremely dilated small bowel loops, which are carefully mobilized using a 30-degree scope and maximum rotation of the operating bed. The loops are fragile and are manipulated gently from the mesentery without direct grasping.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the small bowel loops to create a proper working space. The ileocecal mesentery is exposed, facilitated by adhesions between the right colon and the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon then dissects, ligates, and sections the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon visualizes the duodenum and iliac artery while performing medial to lateral mobilization of the right mesocolon. The right ureter is also identified during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the distal mesentery and intracorporally resects the ileum using a flexible endostapler. Adhesions between the omentum, right colon, and anterior abdominal wall are divided, as well as those between the liver and proximal transverse colon. The hepatic flexure is mobilized from both superior and inferior approaches.
The surgeon overturns and suspends the specimen toward the right hypochondrium, achieving full mobilization of the right colon. The lower duodenal knee is visualized in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs colonic resection at the level of the proximal transverse colon using a flexible endostapler. Both ileal and colonic resections are completed intracorporally, facilitating a smaller mini-incision.

The surgeon places the completely freed specimen in the right hypochondrium. The distal ileal stump is retrieved, and a mini incision is laparoscopically positioned over the colic stump. The specimen is then extracted, and a proper mesocolic excision is performed.

The surgeon performs an extracorporeal ileocolic anastomosis, hand-sewn in a side-to-side, isoparastaltic manner. The small bowel, visibly distended, is carefully exteriorized and then reintroduced into the abdominal cavity to prevent internal hernia and ensure the correct positioning of the anastomosis behind the dilated loops.
